title: Nullification
orientation: Abolitionists Rising's pillar page on nullification: states are not bound to enforce immoral or unconstitutional federal decrees like Roe v. Wade, and they are morally and legally obligated to interpose between tyrants and the victims of legalized murder.
audiences: pro-life leader who defers to the Supreme Court
state legislator weighing an abolition bill

questions.question: Why do abolitionists say Roe v. Wade was 'null and void' the moment it was handed down, rather than a binding law that had to wait until 2022 to be undone?
questions.answer: Because no government has authority to legalize child sacrifice. A decree that purports to authorize mass murder is not a law at all; it is invalid from the moment it is uttered, and the states sinned by complying with it for fifty years.
questions.quote: As abolitionists, we believe that no government possesses the rightful authority to legalize child sacrifice. The moment Supreme Court Justice Harry Blackmun delivered the majority decision in *Roe v. Wade*, the decision was legally null and void and should have been treated as such. By complying with the Supreme Court’s order to allow mass murder for 50 years, the states made themselves complicit in the murder of more than 65 million human beings.

questions.question: With Roe overturned, why is the doctrine of nullification still relevant in 2025?
questions.answer: Federal Democrats have openly stated they intend to codify Roe by statute, pack the Court, or both, and pro-abortion states are mandating legal abortion of all their municipalities. Counties, cities, and states must therefore be ready to defy any future federal or state-level abortion mandate.
questions.quote: With *Roe* now overturned, there isn’t presently the same need for state-level nullification of federal decrees, but there very well could be in the not-too-distant future. Federal democrats have made clear their desire to pass a law codifying *Roe*, stacking the Supreme Court with abortion supporters, or both. We must be ready to defy tyrants once again if the time comes. Further, county- or city-level nullification might need to be practiced in states that mandate legal abortion of all its municipalities.

questions.question: What is the underlying authority dispute that nullification really turns on?
questions.answer: Decades of propaganda have trained Americans to treat the Supreme Court as the ultimate, unappealable arbiter of every legal conflict. Nullification challenges that premise by asking which authority — God's law, the Constitution, or nine unelected justices — actually has the final word.
questions.quote: Nullification relates to a dispute of authority: Who is legally authorized to do what, and what is the standard in legal conflict? Through decades of propaganda, many have come to believe that the federal government, and the Supreme Court especially, is the standard in any legal conflict – that its authority is ultimate and unassailable.

questions.question: How does the article argue that submitting to the Supreme Court has become an act of idolatry inside the pro-life movement?
questions.answer: When leaders like Joe Pojman compare obeying SCOTUS to obeying gravity, they are confessing what they actually worship. Allegiance to God is reduced to a fair-weather alliance that holds only when His word happens to coincide with the Court's opinions.
questions.quote: This view was most precisely articulated by Texas Alliance for Life Executive Director Joe Pojman who told the Austin Chronicle in April, 2019 that “We could no sooner ignore SCOTUS than the force of gravity.” The problem with Pojman’s statement is much bigger than politics. In equating obedience to the Supreme Court with a law of nature, Pojman is telling us who his god is. His allegiance to the true and living God is reduced to a temporary alliance whenever God’s word happens to match the Supreme Court’s opinion.

questions.question: What constitutional and biblical grounds does the article give for ignoring Roe outright?
questions.audience: state legislator weighing an abolition bill
questions.answer: Article VI says only the Constitution and laws made pursuant to it count as legitimate law, and Roe is neither. The Bible commands believers to rescue those being led to slaughter, bring justice to the fatherless, and love their neighbors — duties that flatly forbid complying with any decree that murder must be legal.
questions.quote: Article VI of the Constitution specifies that only the Constitution and the laws made pursuant to it are legitimate laws. Roe is neither a law nor in any way pursuant to the Constitution. As former dean of Stanford Law John Hart Ely put it, “[Roe v Wade] is not constitutional law and gives almost no sense of an obligation to try to be.”

questions.question: What did Thomas Jefferson actually say about treating the Supreme Court as the final arbiter of constitutional questions?
questions.answer: In his 1820 letter to Charles Jarvis, Jefferson called judicial supremacy 'a very dangerous doctrine' that would deliver the country to an oligarchy. He insisted the Constitution made all branches co-equal and co-sovereign precisely so no single tribunal could become despotic.
questions.quote: “You seem to consider the judges as the ultimate arbiters of all constitutional questions; a very dangerous doctrine indeed, and one which would place us under the despotism of an oligarchy. Our judges are as honest as other men, and not more so. They have, with others, the same passions for party, for power, and the privilege of their corps…. Their power [is] the more dangerous as they are in office for life, and not responsible, as the other functionaries are, to the elective control. The Constitution has erected no such single tribunal, knowing that to whatever hands confided, with the corruptions of time and party, its members would become despots. It has more wisely made all the departments co-equal and co-sovereign within themselves.”

questions.question: Did any Founder explicitly endorse nullification as the constitutional remedy?
questions.answer: Yes. Jefferson wrote the Kentucky Resolution declaring that every state has a natural right to nullify, and that nullification is the rightful remedy when the federal government oversteps the Constitution.
questions.quote: Jefferson also endorsed the constitutionality of nullification explicitly in the Kentucky Resolution, in which Jefferson wrote that “Every state has a natural right to nullify,” and that “nullification… is the rightful remedy” to Constitutional infractions committed by the Federal Government.

questions.question: Even honest legal scholars on the left admit Roe was indefensible — what does that tell us?
questions.audience: pro-life leader who defers to the Supreme Court
questions.answer: Liberal jurisprudes from John Hart Ely to Laurence Tribe to Edward Lazarus have conceded Roe is essentially indefensible as constitutional interpretation. The 'right' to abortion was invented by the 1973 Court, which means submitting to it was never about respecting law — it was about fearing men.
questions.quote: In addition to Ely, leftist and Harvard Law professor Laurence Tribe wrote of Roe v Wade, “Behind its own verbal smokescreen, the substantive judgement on which it rests is nowhere to be found.” Edward Lazarus, a former clerk of Roe author Harry Blackmun who “loved Blackmun like a grandfather,” said “as a matter of constitutional interpretation, even most liberal jurisprudes — if you administer truth serum — will tell you [Roe] is basically indefensible.”

questions.question: What if the Constitution itself sanctioned an evil — should it still be obeyed?
questions.answer: No. Garrison was right to burn a copy of the Constitution on July 4, 1854 because of its slavery compromises. A legal document that authorizes the dehumanization and brutalization of image bearers of God is, in his words, a covenant with death and an agreement with hell — and must be defied.
questions.quote: Slavery Abolitionist William Lloyd Garrison was right to burn a copy of the US Constitution during a July 4th, 1854 speech to the Massachusetts Anti-Slavery Society because of the pre- 13th and 14th amendment compromises which allowed for slavery. A legal document which allows for the complete dehumanization and brutalization of image bearers of God is, as Garrison called it, “a covenant with death and agreement with hell.”

questions.question: What about the practical objection that defying the federal government would cost states their funding?
questions.audience: state legislator weighing an abolition bill
questions.answer: The article identifies this as fear-driven idolatry. Pro-life leaders cite lost Medicaid, education funds, and disaster relief to justify continued compliance, but they fear what the Supreme Court — which has no enforcement power — might do more than they fear what God will do to a nation that refuses to outlaw murder.
questions.quote: Again, fear is the driving force here. Fear of what the Supreme Court, which actually has no enforcement power, will do to a state which defies it, rather than fear of what God will do to a state and nation that defies Him.

questions.question: Is there American precedent for states nullifying a federal decree on behalf of human life?
questions.audience: state legislator weighing an abolition bill
questions.answer: Yes. Wisconsin, Michigan, Vermont, New York, and Massachusetts all interposed on behalf of fugitive slaves by nullifying the federal Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 and the federal courts demanding its enforcement. Abolitionists Rising is asking states to do the same for the preborn today.
questions.quote: State governments in Wisconsin, Michigan, Vermont, New York, and Massachusetts interposed on behalf of the victims of slavery by nullifying the federal Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 and the federal courts which demanded its enforcement. We are asking state governments to do the same today.

questions.question: What concrete duty does the doctrine of interposition place on state governors, legislators, and judges?
questions.answer: They are morally and legally obligated to put themselves between the tyrant and the victims being deprived of their God-given right to life. The only appropriate response to an order to allow mass murder is non-compliance and resistance — ignoring Roe and abolishing abortion outright.
questions.quote: With *Roe* being morally execrable and legally null, state governors, legislators, and judges are morally and legally obligated to *interpose* – to put themselves between the tyrant and the victims who are being deprived of their God-given right to life.

related.title: Are Nullification’s Advocates the “Villains” of American History?
related.reason: Direct historical defense of nullification — answers the smear that nullifiers were Confederate villains by surfacing the abolitionist nullifiers (Garrison, Joshua Glover rescuers) who used it against the Fugitive Slave Act.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
related.reason: The flagship case study of state-level nullification: SB13 was Oklahoma's bill to abolish abortion outright by ignoring Roe rather than crafting incremental legislation that begs the Court for permission.

related.title: Contrary to Treat's Media Tour, SB13 is Not a Secession Bill
related.reason: Explicitly tagged with 'Nullification' and 'SB13' — refutes the common confusion that nullifying an unjust federal decree is the same as seceding from the Union.

related.title: Anti-SB13 Campaigners Aren't Ignorant. They're Trying to Deceive You.
related.reason: Long-form rebuttal to the same pro-life leadership establishment this pillar accuses of Supreme Court idolatry, showing how anti-nullification arguments are deployed in practice.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Expands on the Joe Pojman 'gravity' quote cited in this pillar, treating uncritical submission to SCOTUS as a religious problem, not merely a strategic one.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Provides the wider abolitionist framework — nullification is the legal mechanism that makes immediate abolition possible instead of waiting decades for incrementalist permission slips from federal courts.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Sibling AR pillar page: where Nullification establishes the right to defy Roe, Equal Protection lays out the constitutional and statutory mechanism (14th Amendment equal protection of the preborn) that nullifying states should enforce.
